description:
You are an on–call incident responder who has just been handed a small JSON data-set that documents the most recent security-related events in the fleet.  
Your goal is to ➊ verify that the data structurally matches the provided JSON-Schema and ➋ extract a subset of “high” severity incidents for further review.

Directory layout that already exists when the session starts  
/home/user/incident_responder/  
├── incidents.json                 ← the raw event feed (array of objects)  
└── incident_schema.json           ← Draft-07 JSON-Schema describing each object

What you must do from the terminal
1. Validate /home/user/incident_responder/incidents.json against /home/user/incident_responder/incident_schema.json.  
   • If the validation succeeds you will later record “Validation: PASS” in the log (see step 3).  
   • You may use any command-line tool that is available inside the container (e.g. jq, ajv, python -m jsonschema, etc.).  
     The automated grader only checks that the final log reports “PASS”, **not** which tool you used.

2. Using jq, create a new file named /home/user/incident_responder/high_severity_incidents.json that meets **all** of the following criteria:  
   a. Contains only the objects whose "severity" field equals the string "high".  
   b. For each object, keep exactly three keys in this order: id, timestamp, message (omit the severity key entirely).  
   c. Sort the resulting array in ascending numeric order by id.  
   d. Pretty-print the JSON (indentation of two spaces, newline after the closing square bracket).

   Example of the required structural format (values will match the source data):  
   [
     {
       "id": 1002,
       "timestamp": "2024-05-21T13:35:44Z",
       "message": "Unauthorized login attempt detected"
     },
     {
       "id": 1004,
       "timestamp": "2024-05-21T13:37:10Z",
       "message": "Suspicious outbound traffic detected"
     }
   ]

3. Create a text log file at /home/user/incident_responder/processing.log with **exactly four lines** (each line terminated by a single UNIX newline \n).  
   The grader compares the file byte-for-byte, so follow the template precisely:

   Line 1 → Validation: PASS  
   Line 2 → Total_Records: 4  
   Line 3 → High_Severity: 2  
   Line 4 → File_Created: high_severity_incidents.json

   Notes:  
   • Do not add extra spaces (there is exactly one space after each colon).  
   • The values 4 and 2 must be computed, not hard-coded, because the grader will cross-check them against the source JSON.

Success criteria that the automated test will verify  
• /home/user/incident_responder/high_severity_incidents.json exists, is valid JSON, is pretty-printed, contains only the required fields, and is sorted by id.  
• /home/user/incident_responder/processing.log exists and contains the four lines *verbatim* as specified above.  
• The counts (Total_Records and High_Severity) reflect the live contents of incidents.json at the moment your commands run.  

You do not need root privileges. All required files are writable by the current user.  
Feel free to echo intermediate output to the console, but ensure the two deliverable files are created exactly as described.  
A typical solution should require no more than three to four commands in total.
truth:
Initial files present before the agent starts:

1. /home/user/incident_responder/incidents.json  
```
[
  {"id": 1001, "timestamp": "2024-05-21T13:33:12Z", "severity": "low",    "message": "Disk usage at 70%"},
  {"id": 1002, "timestamp": "2024-05-21T13:35:44Z", "severity": "high",   "message": "Unauthorized login attempt detected"},
  {"id": 1003, "timestamp": "2024-05-21T13:36:00Z", "severity": "medium", "message": "CPU load exceeds threshold"},
  {"id": 1004, "timestamp": "2024-05-21T13:37:10Z", "severity": "high",   "message": "Suspicious outbound traffic detected"}
]
```

2. /home/user/incident_responder/incident_schema.json  
```
{
  "$schema": "http://json-schema.org/draft-07/schema#",
  "type": "array",
  "items": {
    "type": "object",
    "required": ["id", "timestamp", "severity", "message"],
    "properties": {
      "id":        { "type": "integer" },
      "timestamp": { "type": "string",  "format": "date-time" },
      "severity":  { "type": "string",  "enum": ["low", "medium", "high"] },
      "message":   { "type": "string" }
    }
  }
}
```

Expected files after the agent successfully completes the task:

1. /home/user/incident_responder/high_severity_incidents.json  
```
[
  {
    "id": 1002,
    "timestamp": "2024-05-21T13:35:44Z",
    "message": "Unauthorized login attempt detected"
  },
  {
    "id": 1004,
    "timestamp": "2024-05-21T13:37:10Z",
    "message": "Suspicious outbound traffic detected"
  }
]
```

2. /home/user/incident_responder/processing.log  
```
Validation: PASS
Total_Records: 4
High_Severity: 2
File_Created: high_severity_incidents.json
```

Both files must be world-readable (`chmod 644`) and owned by the current user.  
The grading harness will parse processing.log and compare its lines exactly; it will also load the JSON file with jq and confirm the array length, key set, sorting order, and pretty-print spacing.
